title: Vocabulary Builder
description: Test your knowledge of 15 essential SAT and GRE vocabulary words with flashcard-style quizzes
viralHook: Think you have a strong vocabulary? Only 3% of people score 15/15 on this quiz

props.title: SAT/GRE Vocabulary Builder
props.description: Test yourself on 15 high-frequency vocabulary words that appear on the SAT and GRE. Select the correct definition for each word. Your score and explanations are shown at the end.

questions.question: UBIQUITOUS
questions.explanation: Ubiquitous (adj.) means existing or being everywhere at the same time. From Latin 'ubique' meaning 'everywhere.' Example: 'Smartphones have become ubiquitous in modern society.'

questions.question: EPHEMERAL

options.label: Lasting for an extremely long time
Lasting for a very short time

questions.explanation: Ephemeral (adj.) means lasting for a very short time; transitory. From Greek 'ephemeros' meaning 'lasting only a day.' Example: 'The ephemeral beauty of cherry blossoms makes them all the more precious.'

questions.question: PRAGMATIC

options.label: Dealing with things sensibly and realistically

questions.explanation: Pragmatic (adj.) means dealing with things sensibly and realistically, in a way that is based on practical rather than theoretical considerations. Example: 'She took a pragmatic approach to solving the budget crisis.'

questions.question: LACONIC
questions.explanation: Laconic (adj.) means using very few words; brief and concise. Named after Laconia (Sparta), whose inhabitants were famous for terse speech. When Philip II of Macedon threatened 'If I invade Laconia, I shall turn you out,' the Spartans replied with one word: 'If.'

questions.question: PERFUNCTORY

options.label: Carried out with minimum effort or reflection
Performed with extreme care and attention

questions.explanation: Perfunctory (adj.) means carried out with a minimum of effort or reflection; done as a routine duty. Example: 'He gave a perfunctory nod before returning to his work.'

questions.question: AMELIORATE

options.label: To make something better; to improve
To combine two things into one

questions.explanation: Ameliorate (v.) means to make something bad or unsatisfactory better; to improve. From Latin 'melior' meaning 'better.' Example: 'The new policies were designed to ameliorate working conditions in the factory.'

questions.question: ENERVATE

options.label: To fill with energy and excitement
To cause someone to feel drained of energy

questions.explanation: Enervate (v.) means to cause someone to feel drained of energy or vitality; to weaken. This is a common trick word -- it looks like 'energize' but means the opposite. Example: 'The oppressive heat enervated the entire team.'

questions.question: EQUIVOCATE

options.label: To use ambiguous language to conceal the truth

questions.explanation: Equivocate (v.) means to use ambiguous language so as to conceal the truth or avoid committing oneself. From Latin 'aequus' (equal) + 'vox' (voice) -- speaking with equal voice on both sides. Example: 'The politician equivocated when asked about the scandal.'

questions.question: SYCOPHANT

options.label: A person who acts obsequiously to gain advantage
A person who lives as a hermit
A musical instrument from ancient Greece

questions.explanation: Sycophant (n.) means a person who acts obsequiously toward someone important in order to gain advantage; a flatterer. Example: 'The CEO surrounded herself with sycophants who never challenged her ideas.'

questions.question: RECALCITRANT

options.label: Having an unwillingness to obey; stubbornly defiant
Tending to repeat the same mistakes

questions.explanation: Recalcitrant (adj.) means having an obstinately uncooperative attitude toward authority or discipline. From Latin 'recalcitrare' meaning 'to kick back.' Example: 'The recalcitrant student refused to follow the classroom rules.'

questions.question: LOQUACIOUS

options.label: Tending to talk a great deal; talkative

questions.explanation: Loquacious (adj.) means tending to talk a great deal; talkative. From Latin 'loqui' meaning 'to speak.' It is essentially the antonym of 'laconic.' Example: 'The loquacious dinner guest dominated every conversation.'

questions.question: OBFUSCATE

options.label: To make clear and easy to understand
To render obscure, unclear, or unintelligible

questions.explanation: Obfuscate (v.) means to render obscure, unclear, or unintelligible; to bewilder. From Latin 'obfuscare' meaning 'to darken.' Example: 'The lawyer attempted to obfuscate the facts of the case with irrelevant details.'

questions.question: SALIENT
questions.explanation: Salient (adj.) means most noticeable or important; prominent; standing out. From Latin 'salire' meaning 'to leap.' The salient points are the ones that 'leap out' at you. Example: 'The report highlighted the most salient findings of the study.'

questions.question: CACOPHONY

options.label: A harsh mixture of discordant sounds
An echo that repeats many times

questions.explanation: Cacophony (n.) means a harsh, discordant mixture of sounds. From Greek 'kakos' (bad) + 'phone' (sound). The opposite is 'euphony' (pleasant sound). Example: 'The cacophony of car horns and construction noise made it impossible to concentrate.'

questions.question: PRESCIENT

options.label: Having knowledge of events before they take place
Lacking any awareness of one's surroundings
Relating to ancient or prehistoric times

questions.explanation: Prescient (adj.) means having or showing knowledge of events before they take place; having foresight. From Latin 'praescire' meaning 'to know beforehand.' Example: 'Her prescient warning about the market crash saved investors millions.'
